did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model named EdgeNeXt-ASCEND, which integrates streamlined self-attention mechanisms and multi-scale features, to classify skin lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, etc.) from dermoscopic images using the ISIC-UFES dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new lightweight deep learning model named EdgeNeXt-ASCEND for skin lesion classification. It details the methodology (including CASS, SDSTA, and SAC modules), describes the construction of the ISIC-UFES dataset from ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20, and reports quantitative results from ablation and comparative experiments (Tables II and III) demonstrating performance metrics like accuracy and F1-score. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE IMCEC), it contains original technical contributions and empirical validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhancing Skin Lesion Classification with Streamlined Self-Attention and Multi-scale Features
journal: 2024 IEEE 6th Advanced Information Management, Communicates, Electronic and Automation Control Conference (IMCEC)
brief_summary: This study proposes EdgeNeXt-ASCEND, a lightweight deep learning model for classifying skin lesions from dermoscopic images to address the high computational costs of existing methods. The architecture integrates streamlined self-attention mechanisms, specifically Combined Activation Symmetric Self-Attention (CASS) and Parallel Convolutional Self-Attention Downsampling (PCSA-DS), with a Stage Aggregated Classifier (SAC) for multi-scale feature fusion. The model was evaluated on the ISIC-UFES dataset, which was constructed by combining the ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets. Performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, specificity, and macro F1-score metrics through ablation and comparative experiments against mainstream lightweight networks. The results demonstrate that EdgeNeXt-ASCEND achieves superior classification performance with 92.61% accuracy and 92.53% F1-score while maintaining lower computational requirements compared to state-of-the-art alternatives.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes EdgeNeXt-ASCEND for classifying skin lesions from dermoscopic images. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin lesion classification' and 'classifying various skin diseases'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion types) using a Stage Aggregated Classifier (SAC) with a fully connected layer and softmax output. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics: accuracy, precision, specificity, and macro F1-score on the ISIC-UFES dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 25,331 high-resolution dermoscopic images encompassing 8 common types of skin lesions. It was used as a source component to construct the ISIC-UFES dataset.

use.training: Source for constructing the ISIC-UFES dataset.
use.validation: Source for constructing the ISIC-UFES dataset.
use.testing: Source for constructing the ISIC-UFES dataset.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of 2,298 skin lesion images captured by various smartphones, containing 6 prevalent types of skin lesions. It was used as a source component to construct the ISIC-UFES dataset.

use.training: Source for constructing the ISIC-UFES dataset.
use.validation: Source for constructing the ISIC-UFES dataset.
use.testing: Source for constructing the ISIC-UFES dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-UFES
datasets_used.description: A constructed dataset created by combining ISIC-2019 and PAD-UFES-20. It contains 18,000 dermoscopic images with 6 classes, balanced via random oversampling and instance hardness threshold undersampling. It was used for all experimental evaluations.

use.training: Used for training the EdgeNeXt-ASCEND model and ablation experiments.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training and evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing and comparative experiments against other lightweight networks.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (accuracy, precision, specificity, F1-score) without statistical significance analysis.

ablation_experiments.description: Results of ablation experiments on the ISIC-UFES dataset comparing the baseline EdgeNeXt-S (Base) and incremental additions of SAC (A), CASS (B), and PCSA-DS (C) modules.
ablation_experiments.notes: The paper reports that adding the SAC module improved accuracy, precision, specificity, and F1-score by 0.89%, 0.94%, 0.18%, and 0.89% respectively compared to the baseline. Adding CASS improved metrics by 0.98%, 1.02%, 0.19%, and 0.97%. Adding PCSA-DS improved metrics by 1.28%, 1.30%, 0.26%, and 1.28% compared to the baseline.

comparative_experiments.description: Comparison of EdgeNeXt-ASCEND against mainstream lightweight networks on the ISIC-UFES dataset.
comparative_experiments.notes: EdgeNeXt-ASCEND achieved the highest accuracy, precision, specificity, and F1-score among all compared models. It maintained a favorable balance between computational cost (FLOPs) and model size (Params).

architecture_details.base_architecture: EdgeNeXt-S (lightweight architecture integrating Vision Transformers and CNNs)

modules.name: Stage Aggregated Classifier (SAC)
modules.description: Expanded classifier for multi-scale feature fusion. Incorporates feature maps from stages 2, 3, and 4 via shortcut connections. Uses layer normalization and concatenation to integrate high-level global information with low-level detail.

modules.name: Compact Attention with Symmetric Sigmoid (CASS)
modules.description: Replaces standard attention to leverage global receptive field while reducing computational overhead. Uses symmetric sigmoid activation.

modules.name: Split Depth-wise Transpose Attention (SDSTA)
modules.description: Novel module introduced to replace the SDTA module in EdgeNeXt to enhance feature extraction.

modules.name: Parallel Convolutional Self-Attention Downsampling (PCSA-DS)
modules.description: Module based on a variant of CASS to preserve information during downsampling. Combines global information (via linear projection and softmax attention) and local information (via convolution on layer-normalized features).

key_components: Shortcut connections for multi-scale feature fusion

training_details.data_augmentation: Random oversampling and instance hardness threshold undersampling for class imbalance mitigation

classification_or_output_head.details: Incorporates feature maps from stages 2, 3, and 4. Features are averaged across channel dimension for dimensionality reduction, normalized, concatenated, and passed to a fully connected layer with softmax output.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CAD framework (encoder-decoder segmentation network with FCN-based DenseNet)
preprocessing_techniques: Random oversampling for class imbalance mitigation
Instance hardness threshold undersampling for class imbalance mitigation
Layer normalization applied to feature maps in the Stage Aggregated Classifier (SAC) module
L2 normalization applied to query (Q) and key (K) vectors in the attention mechanism
Averaging feature maps across the channel dimension for dimensionality reduction in the SAC module
Concatenation of processed feature maps from stages 2, 3, and 4 in the SAC module

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for class imbalance mitigation in the construction of the ISIC-UFES dataset.
Used in combination with random oversampling for class imbalance mitigation in the construction of the ISIC-UFES dataset.

conclusions: The proposed EdgeNeXt-ASCEND model achieves superior classification performance on the ISIC-UFES dataset with 92.61% accuracy and 92.53% macro F1-score, outperforming mainstream lightweight networks such as MobileViT-V1-S, SwiftFormer-L, and EfficientNet-B1.

EdgeNeXt-ASCEND maintains a favorable balance between computational efficiency and accuracy, requiring only 5.295 million parameters and 972 million FLOPs, which are lower than or comparable to other high-performing lightweight models.

The integration of the Stage Aggregated Classifier (SAC) module improves accuracy, precision, specificity, and F1-score by 0.89%, 0.94%, 0.18%, and 0.89% respectively compared to the baseline EdgeNeXt-S, by effectively fusing global high-level features with local low-level spatial information.

The Combined Activation Symmetric Self-Attention (CASS) module enhances the model's ability to locate and identify lesion areas, increasing sensitivity to key features and reducing misidentification risks, resulting in metric improvements of 0.98%, 1.02%, 0.19%, and 0.97% over the baseline.

The Parallel Convolutional Self-Attention Downsampling (PCSA-DS) module effectively preserves informative features during downsampling, leading to metric improvements of 1.28%, 1.30%, 0.26%, and 1.28% compared to the baseline.

The combined EdgeNeXt-ASCEND architecture demonstrates that simplifying the attention mechanism and expanding the classifier allows for effective skin lesion classification with reduced computational costs and memory demands, making it suitable for mobile or embedded devices.
important_remarks: The study relies on a constructed dataset (ISIC-UFES) created by combining ISIC2019 and PAD-UFES-20, rather than using a standard, widely recognized benchmark dataset directly, which may limit comparability with other studies.

The ISIC-UFES dataset was artificially balanced (3000 samples per class) using random oversampling and instance hardness threshold undersampling, which may not reflect real-world class imbalances and could lead to overoptimistic performance estimates.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between EdgeNeXt-ASCEND and the comparative models.
The dataset includes images captured by various smartphones (from PAD-UFES-20), introducing potential variability in image quality and lighting conditions that may affect generalizability.

The paper is published in a conference proceedings (IEEE IMCEC), which typically has less rigorous peer review standards compared to top-tier journals, warranting caution in interpreting the robustness of the claims.
The model was evaluated only on the constructed ISIC-UFES dataset; there is no external validation on independent datasets to assess generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper does not specify the train/validation/test split ratios or methodology for the ISIC-UFES dataset, raising potential concerns about data leakage or non-standard evaluation protocols.
The study focuses on computational efficiency (parameters and FLOPs) but does not report inference time or latency, which are critical metrics for deployment on mobile or embedded devices as claimed.
